name: Nintendo Entertainment System
description: The NES is the 8-bit home console that brought classic game franchises into living rooms worldwide.
funFact: In Japan, the NES was called the Famicom and had a microphone in the controller.
whyLearn: It's a classic platform to understand how games work under the hood.

codingExamples.title: Change Background Color (6502 Assembly)
codingExamples.code:
; Set background color to blue
LDA #$01     ; Load value 1 into A
STA $2001    ; Store A at PPU Mask register
codingExamples.beginnerExplanation: This code changes the NES's background color. It loads a number into the computer's 'A' box, then tells the screen to use that color.
explanation: LDA #$01 loads the number 1 into the accumulator.
STA $2001 puts that number into the hardware's screen setting register (PPU Mask).

websites.title: NESDev Wiki
websites.description: Comprehensive technical documentation for NES development.

websites.title: Making NES Games
websites.description: Simple online 6502 assembler and tutorials.

name: Super Nintendo Entertainment System
description: The SNES was Nintendo’s 16-bit powerhouse for colorful, rich games and legendary sound.
funFact: The SNES sound chip was made by Sony!
whyLearn: Great for exploring hardware graphics and sound.

codingExamples.title: Set Brightness (65c816 Assembly)
codingExamples.code:
; Set display brightness
LDA #$0F    ; 0 = off, $0F = full
STA $2100   ; Write to INIDISP register
codingExamples.beginnerExplanation: This tells the Super Nintendo to make the screen bright. It loads a number into a register, and sends it to the screen controller.
explanation: LDA #$0F loads the number 15 (full brightness).
STA $2100 sends it to the INIDISP (screen control) register.

websites.title: Super Famicom/SNES Dev Wiki
websites.description: Community SNES homebrew and development docs.

name: Nintendo 64
ram: 4 MB (up to 8 MB w/ Expansion Pak)
description: Nintendo's entry into 3D introduced analog controls, Mario 64, and a new era of gaming.
funFact: The N64 was the last major console to use cartridges.
whyLearn: A great platform for diving into 3D graphics programming.

codingExamples.title: Print to Debug Console (C, libultra SDK)
codingExamples.beginnerExplanation: This prints a message (“Hello, N64 world!”) using Nintendo’s N64 programmer tools. You see the output on the PC, not TV.
explanation: #include – brings in the special code for N64 development.
mainproc is your main game function.
osSyncPrintf prints a line to the debug output window.

websites.title: N64 Dev Wiki
websites.description: Documentation and tutorials for developing on the Nintendo 64.

name: PlayStation
description: Sony’s first console brought 3D gaming and iconic games like Final Fantasy VII and Metal Gear Solid.
funFact: It was first planned as a Nintendo product, before becoming Sony's.
whyLearn: Good to learn about early 3D and console hardware programming.

codingExamples.title: Print Text (C, PSY-Q SDK)
codingExamples.beginnerExplanation: This prints the words 'Hello, PlayStation!' to the screen by using PS1’s text system from the SDK.
explanation: Loads font graphics into memory.
Opens a text area at the top of the screen.
Prints 'Hello, PlayStation!' in that area.

websites.title: PSXDev
websites.description: Resources and tools for PlayStation 1 homebrew and development.

name: Sega Genesis
description: Sega’s 16-bit game system was the home of Sonic the Hedgehog and Rapid Arcade Action.
funFact: Named 'Mega Drive' everywhere but North America.
whyLearn: You learn low-level graphics and music programming for fun games.

codingExamples.title: Print Message (C, SGDK)
codingExamples.beginnerExplanation: This puts 'Hello, Genesis!' on the screen at position 10,13 and keeps it showing.
explanation: Includes Genesis library.
Draws text at (10, 13).
Infinite loop keeps program from ending.

websites.title: SGDK Documentation (Genesis C kit)
websites.description: Official SGDK repository, tools and docs for Genesis development.

name: Xbox
description: Microsoft's first console combined PC components with console gaming and popularized online play.
funFact: The original Xbox controller was nicknamed 'The Duke' for its large size.
whyLearn: Shows how consoles and PCs share common programming ideas.

codingExamples.title: Basic Hello (C, XDK/SDLx)
codingExamples.beginnerExplanation: This prints a message on the Xbox debug screen—like saying 'I’m alive!'
explanation: Includes debug output.
Prints 'Hello, Xbox!'
Infinite loop keeps it running.

websites.title: Xbox Dev Wiki
websites.description: Extensive Xbox hardware and homebrew info.

name: Nintendo GameCube
description: Nintendo’s purple cube played host to Super Smash Bros. and Mario Sunshine.
funFact: Its handle let you 'carry it to a friend’s house!'
whyLearn: Good for learning about modern embedded systems and graphics.

codingExamples.title: Print to Screen (C, libogc/devkitPPC)
codingExamples.beginnerExplanation: This sets up the screen and writes 'Hello, GameCube!' for you to see.
explanation: Initializes video hardware.
Sets up a console area for text.
Prints message.
Infinite loop to keep message showing.

websites.title: DevKitPro
websites.description: Toolchains and docs for GameCube and Wii homebrew.

name: Game Boy
description: The original handheld hit, made for playing on the go with Tetris and Pokémon.
funFact: It could last up to 30 hours on 4 AA batteries!
whyLearn: Ideal for learning fundamentals of low-level programming.

codingExamples.title: Infinite Loop (Z80 Assembly, RGBDS)
codingExamples.code:
; Keep program running forever
start:
    jp start
codingExamples.beginnerExplanation: This keeps the Game Boy busy forever so it doesn’t crash. All real games use something similar for their main loop.
explanation: start: is a label.
jp start jumps back to start—endless loop.

websites.title: GBDev Wiki
websites.description: Everything you ever wanted to know about Game Boy programming.

name: Game Boy Advance
description: A pocket-size 32-bit powerhouse capable of SNES-style and advanced games.
funFact: You could play old Game Boy games on it.
whyLearn: Classic for learning embedded C on real hardware.

codingExamples.title: Fill Screen With Blue (C, devkitARM)
codingExamples.beginnerExplanation: This paints every pixel blue. It sets up the screen and fills it with the color's special number.
explanation: Set display into a mode that allows direct pixel writing.
Loop through all (240x160) screen pixels.
Write blue color value (0x1F) in each pixel.
Infinite loop keeps screen painted.

websites.title: Tonc GBA Guide
websites.description: Legendary GBA programming tutorial.

name: Nintendo DS
description: Dual screens and touch controls made the DS a handheld revolution.
funFact: The Nintendo DS is the best-selling handheld in history.
whyLearn: Explore dual-screen and touchscreen programming.

codingExamples.title: Print Text (C, libnds/devkitPro)
codingExamples.beginnerExplanation: Sets up the screen and prints out “Hello, DS!” as a friendly greeting.
explanation: Initializes text output hardware.
Prints message.
Waits for vertical refresh in a loop.

websites.title: libnds Tutorials
websites.description: Guides and docs for DS development in C.

name: PlayStation Portable
description: Sony's portable powerhouse for 3D games, media, and homebrew.
funFact: Thousands of homebrew games and emulators exist for PSP.
whyLearn: Good for learning modern game console coding.

codingExamples.title: Print Message (C, PSP SDK)
codingExamples.beginnerExplanation: Sets up the screen, prints a welcome message, and keeps it there.
explanation: Initializes the debug screen.
Prints 'Hello, PSP!'.
Sleeps thread so message stays visible.

websites.title: PSP Programming Wiki
websites.description: Community SDK docs and homebrew programming.

name: Cisco IOS Router
description: The world's most popular network routers, essential to the internet.
funFact: You can still use 1980s commands today.
whyLearn: Teaches essentials of real-world networking and automation.

codingExamples.title: Set Hostname
codingExamples.code:
configure terminal
hostname Router01
end
codingExamples.beginnerExplanation: This lets you set your router's name to 'Router01', so you won't get confused between different routers.
explanation: Go into configuration mode.
Change the router's hostname.
Finish configuration.

websites.title: Cisco CLI Analyzer
websites.description: Interactive online tool for learning Cisco CLI commands.

name: Linksys WRT54G Router
description: A legendary Wi-Fi router loved by home hackers for its mod-friendly hardware.
funFact: You can turn it into a powerful firewall or VPN box with free firmware.
whyLearn: It's great for learning networking commands and real-world Linux.

codingExamples.title: Blink Power Light (DD-WRT shell)
codingExamples.code:
# Makes the power LED blink on a WRT54G running DD-WRT
 echo blink > /proc/diag/led/power
codingExamples.beginnerExplanation: This line tells the router's built-in lights to flash, proving you can talk to its hardware!
explanation: echo blink: outputs the word blink.
>/proc/diag/led/power: sends it to the LED control, making the light blink.

websites.title: DD-WRT Wiki
websites.description: Full guides for modding and programming Linksys WRT54G routers.

name: Raspberry Pi as Router
description: A tiny computer that can become a full-featured home, lab, or classroom router.
funFact: A Pi can run as an enterprise-grade firewall thanks to software like OpenWRT.
whyLearn: A hands-on, DIY way to learn about real-world networking hardware and Linux tools.

codingExamples.title: Enable Routing (Linux shell)
codingExamples.code:
# Turns on routing so your Pi can move data between networks
 echo 1 > /proc/sys/net/ipv4/ip_forward
codingExamples.beginnerExplanation: This tells your Pi to start passing internet traffic between your devices, a key router job.
explanation: echo 1: says 'turn it on'.
>/proc/sys/net/ipv4/ip_forward: sends the command to Linux's network settings.

websites.title: Pi My Life Up: Pi Router
websites.description: Step-by-step guide to building a router with Raspberry Pi.

name: Commodore 64
description: The most popular home computer ever, made for fun, games, and learning how to code at home.
funFact: The C64's sound chip is still used for music today.
whyLearn: You write code and immediately see results, making it perfect for complete beginners!

codingExamples.title: Say Hello (Commodore BASIC)
codingExamples.code:
10 REM This is a comment. Lines can have numbers!
20 PRINT "HELLO, WORLD!"   : REM Print something to the screen
30 END                     : REM Program stops here
codingExamples.beginnerExplanation: Each step is written out like a recipe. Type this in and the computer talks back!
explanation: Line 10: A comment—the computer ignores this.
Line 20: PRINT shows your message on screen.
Line 30: END tells the computer you’re done.

websites.title: C64 Wiki
websites.description: Huge resource for C64 hardware and programming help.

name: Apple II
description: The Apple II made home and school computing mainstream, letting anyone code their own games and programs.
funFact: The original used cassette tapes to store programs.
whyLearn: One of the easiest and most fun platforms for classic coding.

codingExamples.title: Say Hello (Integer BASIC)
codingExamples.code:
10 REM This line is a comment
20 PRINT "HELLO APPLE II!"
30 END
codingExamples.beginnerExplanation: Exactly like talking to your computer—every line is a single easy idea.
explanation: 10: Ignore this line, it’s a note.
20: Print words to the screen.
30: End the program.

websites.title: Apple2Online
websites.description: Guides and docs for Apple II programming in BASIC.

name: Raspberry Pi
description: A tiny, affordable computer that can do everything—from programming to making robots.
funFact: You can build an arcade, a smart speaker, or a robot with a Raspberry Pi.
whyLearn: It’s cheap, safe to experiment with, and you can literally make anything you imagine.

codingExamples.title: Say Hello (Python 3)
codingExamples.code:
# This is a comment - the computer ignores this line
print("Hello, world!")  # This shows a message on your screen
codingExamples.beginnerExplanation: Write exactly what you want the computer to say, and it does! Comments (with #) are just notes for humans.
explanation: # Comment for you, not the computer.
print(...) shows whatever you type in quotes.
# We can add comments after code, too.

codingExamples.title: Blink an LED (Python, GPIO Zero)
codingExamples.code:
# This will blink an LED plugged into GPIO pin 17
from gpiozero import LED   # Let us control the LED
from time import sleep     # For waiting
led = LED(17)             # Use pin 17
while True:               # Do this forever
    led.on()                # Turn LED on
    sleep(1)                # Wait 1 second
    led.off()               # Turn LED off
    sleep(1)                # Wait 1 second
codingExamples.beginnerExplanation: Each step is simple: set up the light, turn it on and off with pauses. The computer repeats until you stop it.
explanation: from gpiozero import LED: We need this to control lights.
from time import sleep: Lets us pause our program.
led = LED(17): We plug our light into GPIO pin 17.
while True: Our forever loop (keeps program running).
led.on(): Light turns on.
sleep(1): Wait for one second.
led.off(): Light turns off.
sleep(1): Wait again before the next blink.

websites.title: Raspberry Pi Projects
websites.description: Official step-by-step code and hardware tutorials.

websites.title: GPIO Zero Documentation
websites.description: The best simple intro to controlling Pi electronics with code.

name: ZX Spectrum
description: The Speccy was the UK’s affordable, colorful home programming starter, loved by a generation.
funFact: Games loaded from cassette tapes with screeching sounds.
whyLearn: Its code is simple and every line makes something happen.

codingExamples.title: Print Message (Sinclair BASIC)
codingExamples.code:
10 PRINT "HELLO, ZX SPECTRUM!"
20 GO TO 10
codingExamples.beginnerExplanation: This code prints 'HELLO, ZX SPECTRUM!' over and over like a screensaver.
explanation: 10: Show message to screen.
20: Loop back to line 10, repeating forever.

websites.title: World of Spectrum
websites.description: Portal for ZX Spectrum software and programming.

name: MAME Arcade
description: MAME is the world-standard emulator for preserving and running classic arcade machines.
funFact: MAME supports over 10,000 different arcade games!
whyLearn: Learn how real arcade machines were programmed and preserved.

codingExamples.title: Add Hello World to Driver (C)
codingExamples.code: machine().logerror("Hello, MAME!\n"); // Write message to emulator output
codingExamples.beginnerExplanation: Lets you add a message to the emulator output, great for debugging your own arcade game driver.
explanation: machine().logerror: Function to print debug text.
"Hello, MAME!\n": The text message.

websites.title: MAME Dev Docs
websites.description: Official documentation for MAME programming and development.

name: Neo Geo
description: The Neo Geo ran powerful arcade and home games with rich sprites and epic fighting games.
funFact: Neo Geo cartridges could be larger than some laptops at the time.
whyLearn: Teaches the real deal: how arcade machines handled graphics and sound.

codingExamples.title: Main Loop (M68000 Assembly)
codingExamples.code:
; Keep running forever
mainloop:
    bra mainloop
codingExamples.beginnerExplanation: Creates an infinite loop (never ending) that every arcade game needs to keep checking for player input.
explanation: mainloop: label for the start.
bra mainloop: Keep going back to mainloop forever.

websites.title: Neo Geo Dev Wiki
websites.description: Comprehensive documentation for Neo Geo programming.
